title: Wood stove installation: Building Regulations Part J
subtitle: What the regulations require, what HETAS covers, and what an installer actually does
excerpt: A wood-burning stove is notifiable work under Building Regulations Part J in England and Wales. You can have it installed by a HETAS-registered engineer who self-certifies, or by any contractor who notifies Building Control. The liner, hearth, and air supply must all comply with the Approved Document.
sourceNotes: Gov.uk Building Regulations Part J Approved Document 2010 (with 2013 amendments) for combustion appliance installation requirements. HETAS (Heating Equipment Testing and Approval Scheme) technical guidance for solid fuel appliances. BS EN 15287-1:2007 for chimney design. Clean Air Act 1993 and Smoke Control Area regulations for England and Wales. DEFRA exemption list for approved stoves in smoke control areas.

content.text: A wood-burning stove is Building Regulations notifiable work in England and Wales (Part J). Have it fitted by a HETAS-registered engineer who self-certifies compliance, or submit a Building Control application and have an inspector sign off the job. There is no other legal route.

Hearth: non-combustible hearth of minimum 840 x 840 mm (with the stove projecting no more than 225 mm from the back wall) or as specified by the stove manufacturer. Minimum 125 mm depth for a constructional hearth; 12 mm for a superimposed hearth on a solid concrete floor.
Clearances to combustibles: minimum 150 mm from the stove body to combustible material at the sides. Minimum 225 mm above the appliance to combustible material above.

Flue liner: the liner diameter must match the stove outlet. A 125 mm liner is standard for stoves up to 6 kW; 150 mm for higher outputs. The liner must run the full chimney height with no joints inside. A flexible stainless steel liner is the standard retrofit choice.

Air supply: the stove needs permanent ventilation air. Stoves up to 5 kW output in a modern airtight house need a dedicated air supply. Older, leakier houses (built before 2008) with high natural ventilation are usually exempt from the air vent requirement if the room volume exceeds the Approved Document limits.

Most UK towns and cities have smoke control areas under the Clean Air Act 1993. In these areas, only government-approved 'exempt' stoves may be used without special authorisation; non-exempt stoves are illegal in smoke control areas regardless of HETAS registration. The approved-stove register is searchable online; approved stoves must burn only dry wood at moisture content below 20%. Check your postcode on the official smoke control area map before specifying a stove.
Surveys the chimney: checks flue diameter, chimney height (minimum 4.5 m from the appliance), stack condition, and whether the pot is suitable.

Installs the liner: a flexible 904-grade stainless steel liner dropped from the chimney top and connected to the stove outlet at the base. The liner is clamped at the top and sealed at the pot with a plate and liner cap.
Fits the register plate: a steel plate that blanks the old fireplace opening around the liner. This stops the chimney acting as an air leakage path when the stove is off.
Positions and connects the stove: sets the stove on the hearth, connects to the liner, commissions the draw, and issues the HETAS certificate.
What you need before booking an installer
Confirmation whether your address is in a smoke control area. Check the official smoke control area map online.
Confirmed stove output (kW): this determines liner diameter and air supply requirements
Access to the chimney top for the liner drop (scaffolding or safe roof access may add cost)
